claims.claim_text: The opening Sarah Park / holiday PagerDuty incident is presented as a specific real-world war story.
claims.anchor_text: Seattle. December 24, 2023. 4:17 PM. Sarah Park was already late to her family's holiday dinner when her phone buzzed with a PagerDuty alert. The e-commerce recommendation system she'd built had just crashed—on the busiest shopping day of the year.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A production recommendation system can fail at the worst possible time, which is why ML teams need automated checks before deployments reach users.
claims.rationale: This is a named, dated, highly specific incident narrative with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a specific causal chain in which a retrained model was 3x slower and caused production timeouts.
claims.anchor_text: The root cause was embarrassingly simple: a well-meaning teammate had deployed a "small improvement" to the model. They'd retrained it on last month's data, saw that accuracy looked good in their Jupyter notebook, and pushed it to production. What they didn't notice was that the new model was 3x slower than the old one. Under holiday traffic, inference latency caused cascading timeouts across the platform.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A teammate retrained the model, checked offline accuracy, and deployed it without noticing that the new model was materially slower under production load.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the exact retraining story and 3x slowdown are unsupported specifics.

claims.claim_text: The story gives precise concurrency, recovery-time, and business-loss figures for the incident.
claims.anchor_text: "But it worked when I tested it!" her teammate protested. That sentence haunts every ML engineer who's heard it. Of course it worked in testing. Everything works in testing. Testing isn't production. Testing doesn't have 50,000 concurrent users. Testing doesn't have the weird edge cases that real traffic surfaces within minutes. The fix took four hours. Sarah missed her family dinner. The company lost an estimated $2 million in sales. All because they had no automated checks between a developer's laptop and production.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Testing environments rarely capture the concurrency, latency, and edge-case behavior of production traffic, so teams need automated checks before a model can move safely from a developer laptop to production.
claims.rationale: This paragraph relies on fabricated-looking quantitative specifics rather than a documented incident.

claims.claim_text: The blockquote is presented as a real conference quotation.
claims.anchor_text: > "Traditional software can break in predictable ways: it compiles or it doesn't, tests pass or they don't. ML models break in subtle ways: they pass tests but give bad predictions, or good predictions but slowly, or fast predictions on training data but slow on production data. CI/CD for ML needs to catch all of it."
claims.suggested_rewrite: > Traditional software failures are often easier to catch with deterministic tests, while ML delivery also needs checks for prediction quality, latency, and serving behavior.
claims.rationale: This is a verbatim quote without a verifiable source.

claims.claim_text: The quote is attributed to Sarah Park at MLConf 2024.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > Generalized from common production ML failure modes.
claims.rationale: The named speaker and event attribution are unsupported.

claims.claim_text: The module continues to refer back to Sarah as if the opening incident were documented fact.
claims.anchor_text: This module teaches you how to build the safety nets Sarah wished she'd had.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This module teaches you how to build the safety nets ML teams need before model changes reach production.
claims.rationale: Once the named war story is removed, this sentence also needs to be generalized.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Actions is described as the dominant CI/CD platform for ML projects.
claims.anchor_text: GitHub Actions has become the dominant CI/CD platform for ML projects, and for good reason. It's free for public repositories, integrates seamlessly with GitHub, and supports the complex workflows that ML requires.
claims.suggested_rewrite: GitHub Actions is a common CI/CD option for ML projects because it is built into GitHub and supports hosted runners, schedules, and matrix workflows.
claims.rationale: The integration and workflow-support points are defensible, but the market-dominance claim is too strong as written.

claims.claim_text: The caching section uses a hard runtime target of keeping CI under 15 minutes.
claims.anchor_text: Because ML repositories pull heavy dependencies (like PyTorch or Hugging Face transformers) and massive model artifacts, caching is not optional—it is a strict requirement to keep CI times under 15 minutes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because ML repositories often pull heavy dependencies and large model artifacts, caching is usually important for keeping CI runtimes practical.
claims.rationale: The cache action supports the general lesson that caching dependencies and build outputs improves workflow time, but the 15-minute target is illustrative.

claims.claim_text: The module states that pipeline YAML is vendor-locked and gives product-specific non-portability examples.
claims.span_hint: section: Portable CI/CD with Dagger, intro paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Here's a frustrating reality of CI/CD: your pipeline YAML is vendor-locked. A GitHub Actions workflow doesn't run on GitLab CI. A GitLab pipeline doesn't run on Jenkins.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many CI systems use their own workflow definitions, which makes pipeline logic harder to move between platforms without adaptation.
claims.rationale: The general portability problem is real, but the exact cross-product incompatibility examples are broader than a single allowlisted source cleanly supports.

claims.claim_text: Dagger lets teams write pipeline logic in languages such as Python, Go, and TypeScript.
claims.span_hint: section: Portable CI/CD with Dagger, intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Dagger's upstream README states that it runs locally and in CI and provides native SDKs including Go, Python, and TypeScript.

claims.claim_text: The TFX bullet claims a 2017 first-public-description milestone and broad lineage across modern MLOps tools.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Google's TFX (TensorFlow Extended) paper in 2017** was the first public description of a complete ML pipeline. It introduced the concept of "pipeline components" that are now standard: data validation, data transformation, training, model analysis, and serving. Every modern MLOps tool traces its lineage to TFX concepts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **TFX is a well-known example of a production ML pipeline framework.** It uses a component-based architecture for tasks such as data validation, transformation, training, evaluation, and serving.
claims.rationale: TFX is real and influential, but the 'first public description' and universal-lineage claims are too sweeping as written.

claims.claim_text: The GitHub Actions pricing bullet gives a blanket 2,000-minute private-repo allowance and derived run-count math.
claims.anchor_text: 2. **GitHub Actions provides 2,000 free minutes per month** for private repos and unlimited minutes for public repos. A typical ML test suite takes 5-15 minutes, meaning you can comfortably run 130-400 automated pipeline checks per month absolutely free.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **GitHub Actions includes free usage tiers** and standard GitHub-hosted runners are free for public repositories. The exact minutes available for private repositories depend on your GitHub plan, runner type, and how heavy your workflows are.
claims.rationale: GitHub's current pricing varies by plan, so the blanket 2,000-minute statement and the derived monthly-run math are too specific as written.

claims.claim_text: The Michelangelo bullet gives a specific throughput figure and a strong claim about automatic model swapping on drift.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Uber's Michelangelo platform processes over 1.5 million predictions per second** in production. Their foundational 2017 architecture whitepaper introduced continuous training systems that instantly swap models if metric drift violates historical bounds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Uber's Michelangelo is a well-known example of an internal ML platform at production scale.** It is often discussed as an example of why mature ML organizations invest in training, deployment, monitoring, and safety systems.
claims.rationale: Uber has public Michelangelo materials, but this exact throughput figure and instant-swap behavior are not supported as written.

claims.claim_text: The Dagger bullet attributes the project to Solomon Hykes in 2022 and adds an unsourced causal story about his insight.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Dagger was created by Solomon Hykes (creator of Docker) in 2022**. His insight was that CI/CD pipelines suffer the exact same fragmentation and environment inconsistency that plagued applications before containerization, driving his choice to push CI into containers themselves.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Dagger emerged in the early 2020s as a portable, container-based CI/CD tool.** Its appeal is that the same pipeline logic can run more consistently across local machines and CI environments.
claims.rationale: The founder/year detail is plausibly real but mixed with an unsourced causal narrative, so the bullet should be rewritten to the underlying portability lesson.

claims.claim_text: The credit-scoring fairness story gives a date, test-coverage figure, and detailed deployment narrative.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, Model That Passed All Tests, first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **New York. March 2024.** A fintech startup had a robust CI/CD pipeline with 94% test coverage. Their credit scoring model passed every automated check: unit tests, data validation, accuracy threshold, latency check.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A model can pass conventional CI checks and still ship harmful behavior if the pipeline only verifies aggregate metrics and basic regression tests.
claims.rationale: This is a specific, unattributed war story with date and quantitative detail.

claims.claim_text: The story then claims the model rejected applicants from certain ZIP codes due to learned geographic bias.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, Model That Passed All Tests, second paragraph
claims.anchor_text: One month after deployment, they discovered the model was systematically rejecting applicants with certain ZIP codes. The model had learned geographic biases from historical data—and none of their tests caught it.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams sometimes discover after deployment that a model performs acceptably overall while still harming specific subgroups or proxy features they never tested explicitly.
claims.rationale: The fairness lesson is valid, but this specific incident narrative is unsupported.

claims.claim_text: The GPU-cost war story is framed around a precise $100,000 headline.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, GPU bill title
claims.rationale: The exact amount is a load-bearing anecdotal number without a source.

claims.claim_text: The GPU-bill story gives a date, duration, cloud-vendor context, and detailed impact narrative.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, GPU bill first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **Seattle. August 2023.** A startup's CI/CD pipeline had a bug: every PR triggered a full model training job on expensive GPU instances. For two weeks, nobody noticed. When the AWS bill arrived, the CTO nearly had a heart attack.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A misconfigured CI pipeline can accidentally trigger expensive retraining jobs on every pull request, especially when GPU stages are not gated by path filters, caching, or budget alerts.
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed war story with precise timing and impact framing.

further_reading.title: MLOps: Continuous delivery and automation pipelines in machine learning
further_reading.why_relevant: Strong vendor reference for CI, CD, CT, validation, and automation patterns in production ML systems.

further_reading.title: GitHub Actions
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the module's GitHub Actions sections because it covers hosted runners, event triggers, and matrix workflows.

further_reading.title: dagger/dagger
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for Dagger's portable CI/CD model, SDK languages, and local-versus-CI workflow execution.

further_reading.title: Continuous Training for Production ML in the TensorFlow Extended (TFX) Platform
further_reading.why_relevant: A concise primary paper on continuous training for production ML and the operational reasons CT exists.

notes: Excluded Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, tables, quiz items, and hands-on exercise command blocks per prompt. I also left high-level pedagogical framing such as analogies and broad CT/CM motivation out of the claim list unless the prose crossed into specific vendor, pricing, or anecdotal factual territory.
